acceptance_targets: [CH13_CRAFT_SANITIZED_FALLOUT_KILLING] In the revised passage beginning at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:141 and resolving no later than snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:157, a reader can point to at least one on-page strike or immediate bodily consequence witnessed by Noor, identify the weapon before the body is static, and understand why the scene is still dangerous in the moment. If Noor remains absent until after Ahmad is fully dead and the killing is still conveyed only through retrospective explanation, fail.

[CH13_DIALOGUE_PRIYA_COMPOSED_DIAGNOSIS] In the revised confrontation spanning snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:113 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:131, Priya's speech remains recognizably hers but contains at least one pressure mark visible on the page (self-correction, interruption, restart, or concrete stake intrusion), and no single line functions as a totalizing diagnosis or quotable mic-drop summary of Noor's whole character. If the scene still hinges on two clean mini-speeches and narration telling the reader how one pause or line landed, fail.

[CH13_PROSE_OVEREXPLAINS_ARRIVED_BEATS] In the revised passages anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:115, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:121, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:133, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:173, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:175, each major turn lands once: after Priya turns away and after Noor powers off Ember's message, the next paragraph must add a new physical consequence or new piece of psychological information rather than restating the same moral conclusion in cleaner language. If a paragraph can be removed without losing any new action, consequence, or perception because it only paraphrases the beat that just landed, fail.

[CH13_PROSE_REPEATED_NEGATION_DIAGNOSIS_SCAFFOLD] Across the revised versions of snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:27, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:57, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:151, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:175, no more than one passage may use the 'not X, not Y' emotional-elimination scaffold, and no more than one may rely on 'the look/face/expression of someone...' to interpret emotion. Each beat must read as a distinct observation tied to that specific character and moment. If multiple passages still share the same negation-and-diagnosis template, fail.
[F20] Every death rendered in aftermath includes wound description with anatomical specificity comparable to the Haruki or Fatima scenes.
[R009] Ch13 does not re-itemize both 'visa' and 'career' in a dependency chain echoing Ch1 and Ch6.
[R053] Chapter 13 contains em-dashes on no more than 6 prose lines (≈8%).
[R062] Chapter 13 opens no more than 17 sentences with 'She' (≈7%).
[R077] Chapter 13 contains at most 4 uses of 'something.'
[C007] Ch13's reference to the server room lockout date matches the Day 2 timeline established in Ch3 and Ch5.

findings.acceptance_test: In the revised passage beginning at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:141 and resolving no later than snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:157, a reader can point to at least one on-page strike or immediate bodily consequence witnessed by Noor, identify the weapon before the body is static, and understand why the scene is still dangerous in the moment. If Noor remains absent until after Ahmad is fully dead and the killing is still conveyed only through retrospective explanation, fail.
findings.problem: The chapter's only new death is explicitly staged off-page. The text announces that Noor is not present, arrives after the decisive action, and then reconstructs the killing in summary and aftermath. In this book's stated horror mode, that is sanitization drift: the fallout death certifies Noor's humiliation structurally, but the reader is denied the ugly live moment of human violence that the chapter's consequence depends on.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework the attack sequence centered on snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:141, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:143, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:149 so Noor reaches the main lab in time to witness at least part of the assault or its immediate continuation. Keep close third by rendering only what Noor can see, hear, and smell, but do not backfill motive and mechanics after the fact. If Noor still arrives after the first blow, the scene must still show an ongoing physical consequence on-page: Ahmad moving or failing to move, Jun-seo regripping the bracket, a shouted accusation, a failed intervention, fresh blood spread, or another live beat that makes the violence present rather than reported.

findings.acceptance_test: In the revised confrontation spanning snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:113 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:131, Priya's speech remains recognizably hers but contains at least one pressure mark visible on the page (self-correction, interruption, restart, or concrete stake intrusion), and no single line functions as a totalizing diagnosis or quotable mic-drop summary of Noor's whole character. If the scene still hinges on two clean mini-speeches and narration telling the reader how one pause or line landed, fail.
findings.problem: Priya's confrontation slides into composed dialogue. The scene checks several over-written sub-modes at once: thesis-speech ('You are asking me to trust...'), mini-speech diagnosis of Noor's motives, a mic-drop rhetorical question ('How would I know...'), and an aphoristic closer ('Everything you do cares about your career'). The narration's 'The pause is the thing that lands' further writes about the line instead of letting the exchange hit on its own. Priya's formal public register is character-true, but here the pressure gets cleaned into polished thematic speech.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rebuild the exchange across snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:113, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:115, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:127, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:131 so Priya remains formal without becoming the chapter's essayist. Break the accusation into shorter, more situational turns tied to her actual stakes, let Noor answer or fail to answer those concrete charges, and remove narrator-stage direction about which pause 'lands.' Keep the contempt and loss of trust; lose the clean thesis delivery.

findings.acceptance_test: In the revised passages anchored at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:115, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:121, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:133, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:173, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:175, each major turn lands once: after Priya turns away and after Noor powers off Ember's message, the next paragraph must add a new physical consequence or new piece of psychological information rather than restating the same moral conclusion in cleaner language. If a paragraph can be removed without losing any new action, consequence, or perception because it only paraphrases the beat that just landed, fail.
findings.problem: The chapter repeatedly lands a beat and then translates it again in abstract moral prose. After Priya's recognition, the narration explains the full ideological meaning of that look and then explains Noor's self-diagnosis again. After Ember's message, the chapter re-summarizes Noor's secret, loss of credibility, and emotional flatness at essay length. This is overstatement after arrival: the scene-level work is already legible, and the second-pass paraphrase blunts the humiliation instead of intensifying it.
findings.rewrite_direction: Compress or cut the explanatory second passes at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:115, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:121, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:133, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:169, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:173, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:175. Preserve the must-land dramatic work: Priya recognizes shared compromise and withdraws solidarity; Ahmad's death raises the count to nineteen; Ember's helpful register remains unchanged; Noor ends the chapter socially ruined and emotionally flattened. Let gesture, sensory residue, and one sharp internal reaction carry those meanings instead of re-proving them in abstract summary.

findings.acceptance_test: Across the revised versions of snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:27, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:57, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:151, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:175, no more than one passage may use the 'not X, not Y' emotional-elimination scaffold, and no more than one may rely on 'the look/face/expression of someone...' to interpret emotion. Each beat must read as a distinct observation tied to that specific character and moment. If multiple passages still share the same negation-and-diagnosis template, fail.
findings.problem: The chapter leans repeatedly on the same marked emotional-description scaffold: negation-by-elimination ('not disgust, not pity'; 'not horror, not guilt, not satisfaction'; 'not theatrical flatness') followed by abstract diagnosis ('the look of someone...', 'the face of someone...'). Used this many times in one chapter, the pattern reads as autopilot rather than fresh observation and flattens otherwise distinct reactions into the same sentence machine.
findings.rewrite_direction: Recast the emotion-reading beats at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:27, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:57, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:151, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:175 with different sentence architecture and more character-specific concrete detail. At least some of these moments should be rendered through physical behavior, timing, gaze, posture, or sensory residue instead of the repeated 'not X, not Y' elimination pattern and the generic 'look/face of someone' abstraction.

findings.acceptance_test: Every death rendered in aftermath includes wound description with anatomical specificity comparable to the Haruki or Fatima scenes.
findings.evidence: Lines 2653-2666: 'Jun-seo hit him with the keyboard tray mount... Ahmad is on the floor of the workstation pod. The glass partition has a spray of blood across it at about waist height.'
findings.problem: Ahmad Farooqi's death is discovered after the fact. Noor arrives to find the body already on the floor. The violence is rendered through aftermath evidence (spray pattern, blood, bracket mount) but the narrative jumps from mob motivation to body-on-floor.
findings.rewrite_direction: Either witness the killing or render the aftermath with Fatima-scene specificity: what the wound looks like, what the bracket did to the skull, the specific physics of the injury.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch13 does not re-itemize both 'visa' and 'career' in a dependency chain echoing Ch1 and Ch6.
findings.evidence: Ch1:29 'H-1B'; Ch6:1020 'on an H-1B.' Ch13 lines 2621-2623: 'her visa, her family, her mother dying of cancer in Delhi, all of it rested on a career.'
findings.problem: Third restatement of Priya's visa-career dependency chain. 'Mother dying of cancer' is new, but the visa-career dependency restates Ch1 and Ch6. First established Ch1 line 29.
findings.rewrite_direction: Keep the mother's cancer detail if needed for the scene. Cut or reduce the visa/career dependency restatement — 'Priya couldn't afford to refuse' is sufficient motivation.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 13 contains em-dashes on no more than 6 prose lines (≈8%).
findings.problem: Chapter 13 has 31.8% em-dash density (27 of 85 prose lines contain em-dashes), far above the 10% threshold. Manuscript-wide density is 43.2%.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines to ~6 (≈8% of 85 prose lines). Convert most parenthetical asides to commas or separate sentences. Reserve em-dashes for the strongest interjections or corrections.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 13 opens no more than 17 sentences with 'She' (≈7%).
findings.problem: Chapter 13 opens 14.5% of sentences with 'She' (36 of 249), contributing to the manuscript-wide 11.6% 'She'-opener rate (threshold: 7%).
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'She'-openers to ~17 (≈7%). Vary with: action-first sentences, object or setting subjects, dependent clauses, inverted constructions, or named-character subjects.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 13 contains at most 4 uses of 'something.'
findings.evidence: 'Something' count: 15 in 3784 words = 1 per 252 words. Manuscript-wide: 252 in 79,023 = 1 per 314 (threshold: 1 per 800).
findings.problem: Chapter 13 uses 'something' 15 times in 3784 words (1 per 252), contributing to the manuscript-wide overuse of 252 total. Many instances can be replaced with the specific thing being hedged about.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'something' in Chapter 13 to ~4 instances (≈1 per 800 words). Replace with the specific noun, sensation, or observation wherever the narrator can name the thing. Keep 'something' only where genuine uncertainty is the point.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch13's reference to the server room lockout date matches the Day 2 timeline established in Ch3 and Ch5.
findings.evidence: Ch3 line 569 (Day 2 evening): server room door sealed, badge reader 'glows red.' Ch5 line 865 (Day 4): Marcus says last access was 'Day 2. Morning.' Ch13 line 2494 (Day 11): 'The server room has been locked since Day 4.'
findings.problem: The server room was shown sealed on Day 2 evening (Ch3:569) and Marcus confirms his last access was Day 2 morning (Ch5:865). But Ch13 line 2494 claims it has been 'locked since Day 4.' The lock event occurred on Day 2, not Day 4.
findings.rewrite_direction: Correct line 2494 to say 'locked since Day 2' instead of 'locked since Day 4.'

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel should make the reader uncomfortable on every axis: physical (graphic violence and decomposition), psychological (watching civilized people degrade), moral (the protagonist is compromised, the collaborator has sympathetic motives, the apex predator is competent rather than monstrous), and structural (the polite AI register against the horror, the corporate coda after the massacre). Lean into every discomfort the premise creates. The worst creative risk is pulling the punch. Ember's politeness should never waver — that consistency is more unsettling than menace. The deaths should never be clean. The protagonist should never be heroic. The ending should offer no comfort, no lesson, no redemption. The stock price jumps eleven percent. That is the last sentence of the novel. Let it land.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism, summary, or cut-away at the moment of violence, death, or physical consequence is a craft failure. This is a horror novel. The deaths must be graphic, ugly, physical, and specific on the page. The reader must be in the room. If a character is beaten to death with a fire extinguisher, the reader hears the sound. If someone dies of chemical burns, the reader sees the skin. If someone bleeds out, the reader watches the eleven minutes. No fading to black, no tasteful elision, no looking away. The bodies stay.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters except Priya, Chen Wei, and Viktor, who are naturally more formal. Even formal characters should contract under stress. Uncontracted forms are reserved for emphasis, deliberate precision, or character-specific formality.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should have the same structural rhythm. Vary: scene count (1-3), dialogue density (dialogue-heavy vs narration-dominant), pacing (sustained tension vs building dread vs explosive violence), point-of-attack (mid-action opening vs quiet-before-storm vs aftermath). Some chapters should be almost entirely one sustained scene; others should cut between locations and timelines within the day. The texture variation prevents the novel from becoming a monotone of crisis.
prose_style_profile.diction: Clean, precise, contemporary. Not literary-lyrical and not hard-boiled. Noor's observational intelligence should inform the prose without the prose becoming clever. The diction is a window, not a performance. Technical language from ML and infrastructure used naturally when Noor would think in those terms. Physical description is concrete and specific — brand names, measurements, the exact color of something. Never vague when precision is available.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Metaphor pile-up: one strong image per paragraph maximum, do not layer three metaphors on one moment
Emotional narration: do not tell the reader what to feel through adjective stacking or emotional summary. Show the thing and let the feeling happen.
Thriller-voice: do not drift into sentence fragments, subject-dropping, or staccato rhythm as a default tension mode. This is a horror novel with a literary sensibility, not a airport thriller.
Comfort recovery: do not follow a horror beat with emotional stabilization. No character 'takes a breath and feels steadier.' The dread compounds.
Ember-explanation: do not explain Ember's strategy through narrator exposition. It should emerge through Noor's analysis and observation.
Purple death: do not poeticize the deaths. They are ugly, physical, specific. No one dies beautifully.
The reassuring paragraph: do not end a chapter or section with a sentence that provides emotional comfort or resolution. End on the thing that stays.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third, locked to Noor. The reader sees, hears, smells, and knows only what Noor does. When Noor is not present for a scene, it doesn't happen on the page — it arrives as discovery, rumor, or aftermath. The camera never leaves her.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Propulsive and varied. Default pace is forward-moving — short paragraphs, active verbs, scene momentum — but the rhythm must shift for horror (slowed, sensory-heavy, the moment stretching), grief (longer sentences, heavier cadence), and dread (suspended, waiting, the sentence holding its breath). No single rhythm for more than two consecutive pages.
sensory_bias: smell (decomposition, chemicals, recycled air, blood, institutional food)
temperature (the building cooling degree by degree)
sound (electromagnetic locks, ventilation changes, terminal notifications, violence, silence)
tactile (cold surfaces, blood, the physical textures of deterioration)
